name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ (٥٥)
```

```english
So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. Plead [Allah] for forgiveness of your sin, and celebrate the praise of your Lord morning and evening. (55)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ ۙ إِن فِى صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ مَّا هُم بِبَـٰلِغِيهِ ۚ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (٥٦)
```

```english
Indeed those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them—there is [an ambition for] greatness in their breasts, which they will never attain. So seek the protection of Allah; indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (56)
```

```arabic
لَخَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٧)
```

```english
The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely more prodigious than the creation of mankind, but most people do not know. (57)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَلَا ٱلْمُسِىٓءُ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٥٨)
```

```english
The blind one and the seer are not equal, neither are the evildoers and those who have faith and do righteous deeds. Little is the admonition that you take! (58)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٩)
```

```english
Indeed the Hour is bound to come; there is no doubt in it. But most people do not believe. (59)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ رَبُّكُمُ ٱدْعُونِىٓ أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِى سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ (٦٠)
```

```english
Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you!’ Indeed those who are disdainful of My worship will enter hell in utter humiliation. (60)
```

## (Commentary)

When He related the story of Moses and his being sent with the truth to Pharaoh and his people, and their disputing the signs of Allah with falsehood and their plotting therein, and His—the Exalted—support for His prophet and His nullification of their guile, and what their affair led to in terms of the failure of their efforts and an evil return—He branched off from that by commanding His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient. He alerted him that the promise of Allah regarding victory is true, and that the guile of his people, their disputing with falsehood, and their arrogance (الاستكبار) — *the act of seeking greatness and refusing to submit to the truth* — against accepting his call will be nullified and will return as a bane upon themselves. They cannot frustrate Allah, and the promised Hour will surely come, and they shall enter Hell abased.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ** "So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true" until the end of the verse. This is a derivation from what preceded regarding the command to take heed in His saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** "Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them?" (40:21), and what was mentioned after it of the story of Moses, the outcome of the affair of the arrogant who dispute with falsehood, and His—the Exalted—support for the truth and its people.

The meaning is: Since the matter is as such, then be patient with the harm of the polytheists and their disputing with falsehood; indeed, the promise of Allah is true and He will fulfill for you what He promised. What is intended by the promise is what occurs shortly before this in His saying: **إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith" (40:51), regarding the promise of victory.

And His saying: **وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ** "and plead for [forgiveness of] your sin." This is a command to him to seek forgiveness (الاستغفار) — *the act of asking Allah to cover and erase one's shortcomings* — for what is considered a sin (الذنب) in relation to him, even if it is not a sin in the sense of disobeying a masterly command, due to his infallibility (العصمة) (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). A discussion regarding the meaning of sin and forgiveness has already preceded at the end of the sixth volume of this book.

There is another meaning for the sin attributed to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) which we shall point out in the commentary (تفسير) at the beginning of *Surah al-Fath*, if Allah—the Exalted—wills. It has also been said: What is intended by his sin (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the sin of his community, for which he was granted intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah*.

And His saying: **وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكارِ** "and celebrate the praise of your Lord morning and evening." That is, glorify Him, the Glorified, accompanied by His praise for His beautiful favors, continuously and successively with the succession of days, or in every morning and evening. His saying "morning and evening" according to the first meaning is a form of metonymy (الكناية).

It has also been said: What is intended by it are the Dawn (الصبح) and Afternoon (العصر) prayers, and that the verse is Medinan.

However, it is established from the narrations—including the reports of the Ascension (المعراج)—that the five daily prayers were all made obligatory in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة). Thus, if the two obligatory prayers were intended here, that would have been in Mecca before the rest of the five prayers were made obligatory.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِ اللهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطانٍ أَتاهُمْ إِنْ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ ما هُمْ بِبالِغِيهِ** "Indeed those who dispute the signs of Allah without any authority that may have come to them—there is nothing in their breasts but an ambition which they will never attain" (40:56). This is a reinforcement of what preceded in the previous verse regarding the command to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient and the comforting of his soul by confirming the promise of victory. Its summary is that these disputers will not reach their desire and shall never reach it; so let not their disputing grieve you, and be at peace regarding them.

Thus, His saying: **إِنْ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ** "there is nothing in their breasts but an ambition [pride]" is a restriction of the cause necessitating their disputing to pride (الكبر). That is, their motive in this is not the seeking of truth or doubt regarding Our signs such that they desire the manifestation of truth, nor do they possess any proof or authority (سلطان) such that they desire to manifest it. Rather, that which is in their breasts and which calls them to dispute is pride, by which they intend to refute the manifest truth.

And His saying: **ما هُمْ بِبالِغِيهِ** "which they will never attain." The pronoun refers to "pride" (الكبر) in consideration of its consequence; for pride is the cause of disputing, and disputing is intended to nullify the truth and erase the true call. The meaning is: they shall not reach their objective and desire from the disputing they engage in due to their pride.

And His saying: **فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ** "So seek refuge in Allah." That is, seek refuge in Allah from them and the pride they possess, just as Moses sought refuge from every arrogant disputer when he said: **وَقالَ مُوسى إِنِّي عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ الْحِسابِ** "Moses said, ‘Indeed I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Reckoning’" (40:27).

And His saying: **إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ** "Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing." That is, the All-hearing of the supplications of His servants, the All-seeing of their needs, and the One Who sees the state they are in, whether in hardship or ease.

His saying, the Exalted: **لَخَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** "The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely greater than the creation of mankind, but most people do not know" (40:57). The *lam* is for an oath (القسم). What is intended by the heavens and the earth is the entirety of the universe. The meaning of the verse, according to what the context provides, is that they shall not reach their desire and they cannot frustrate Allah; for Allah, Who was capable of creating the entirety of the universe and was not frustrated by it despite its magnitude, is not frustrated by a small part of it, which is the humans who are created and are easier for Him. However, most people are ignorant (جاهلون); they think in their ignorance that they can frustrate Allah through a dispute they wage or any guile they plot.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ** "The blind and the seeing are not equal" (40:58) until the end. When He mentioned that most people do not know, He reinforced it by stating that they are not of the same type. For among them is the blind and the seeing, and they are not equal. He then conjoined to them "those who have faith and do righteous deeds" and "the evildoer." The first group are people of insight (بصيرة) through which they take heed, while the second are those whose hearts Allah has blinded, so they do not take heed.

And His saying: **قَلِيلاً ما تَتَذَكَّرُونَ** "Little is the admonition that you take." This is an address to the people for the purpose of rebuke, and this is the reason for the transition (الالتفات) from the third person to the second person.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَآتِيَةٌ لا رَيْبَ فِيها وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يؤْمِنُونَ** "The Hour is bound to come: there is no doubt about it, but most people do not have faith" (40:59). Allah reminded them in this verse of the coming of the Hour, and in the following verse of His Lordly call to them to supplicate to Him and worship Him, just as the believer from the family of Pharaoh alerted them in the previous story to the coming of the Hour and that the call belongs to Allah, while their deities have no call in this world nor in the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَ رَبُّكُمُ ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ** "Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you [respond to you]’" (40:60). This is a call from Him, the Exalted, to His servants to supplicate to Him, and a promise of response (الاستجابة). He has mentioned the call, the supplication (الدعاء), and the response in absolute terms. We have discussed the meaning of supplication and response extensively under His saying, the Exalted: **أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ** "I answer the solicitor’s invocation when he visits Me with his prayer" (2:186) in the first volume of this book.

And His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ** "Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter hell, abased." **الدخور** (Abasement) means humiliation (الذلة). He has substituted "supplication" with "worship" (عبادة), which indicates that supplication is worship.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Sahifah al-Sajjadiyyah*: **وقلت : «ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْـإِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِيـسَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ» فسميت دعاءك عبادة وتركه استكبارا ـ وتوعدت على تركه دخول جهنم داخرين.** — "And You said: 'Call Me, and I will respond to you; indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter Hell, abased.' Thus, You named Your supplication 'worship' and its abandonment 'arrogance,' and You threatened for its abandonment the entry into Hell, abased."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Hammad ibn 'Isa, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: **سمعته يقول : ادع ولا تقل : قد فرغ من الأمر ـ فإن الدعاء هو العبادة إن الله عز وجل يقول : «إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِيـسَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ» وقال : «ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ».** — "I heard him saying: 'Supplicate and do not say: the matter has already been decided. For indeed, supplication is worship; indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, says: "Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter Hell, abased," and He said: "Call Me, and I will respond to you."'"

I say: His saying (peace be upon him): "For indeed, supplication..." until "...abased" is an argument for what he first exhorted toward in his saying: "Supplicate." And his saying: "And He said: 'Call Me, and I will respond to you'" is an argument for what he said secondly: "And do not say: the matter has already been decided." This is why he (peace be upon him) presented the end of the verse over its beginning in his explanation.

In *al-Khisal*, from Mu'awiyah ibn 'Ammar, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: **يا معاوية من أعطي ثلاثة لم يحرم ثلاثة : من أعطي الدعاء أعطي الإجابة ، ومن أعطي الشكر أعطي الزيادة ـ ومن أعطي التوكل أعطي الكفاية ـ فإن الله عز وجل يقول في كتابه : «وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ» وقال : «لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ» ، وقال : «ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ».** — "O Mu'awiyah, whoever is given three things is not deprived of three: whoever is given [the success to] supplication is given the response; whoever is given [the success to] gratitude is given increase; and whoever is given [the success to] trust (التوكل) — *the act of relying solely upon Allah* — is given sufficiency. For Allah, Mighty and Majestic, says in His Book: 'And whoever trusts in Allah, He is sufficient for him' (65:3), and He said: 'If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing]' (14:7), and He said: 'Call Me, and I will respond to you' (40:60)."

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of narration to Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: **قال قوم للصادق عليه‌السلام : ندعوه فلا يستجاب لنا. قال : لأنكم تدعون من لا تعرفونه.** — "A group said to al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): 'We supplicate to Him but we are not answered.' He said: 'Because you supplicate to One Whom you do not know.'"

I say: We have already cited a number of narrations regarding supplication under His saying: **أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ** "I answer the solicitor’s invocation when he visits Me with his prayer" (2:186) in the first volume of this book.
